definition_plain: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how AI applications connect to external data sources and tools. Instead of every AI app building custom integrations for every service, MCP provides a universal plug-and-socket system — any MCP-compatible AI can talk to any MCP server. It's the USB-C of AI integrations: one connector instead of a drawer full of proprietary cables.

scenario.meeting_line: Instead of building a custom Slack integration, let's just expose an MCP server — then any AI client can connect to it.
scenario.smart_followup: Are we using stdio transport for local tools or streamable HTTP for the hosted ones? That affects our deployment model.

quiz_question.question: Your team builds a code review tool that 5 different AI assistants need to access. Instead of writing 5 custom integrations, you implement one MCP server. What problem does this solve?
options: A) It makes the model faster at generating code
B) It provides a standard interface so any MCP-compatible AI can connect
C) It encrypts all communication between AI models
D) It reduces the model's token usage
quiz_question.explanation: MCP standardizes the connection protocol, so one server implementation works with any compatible client.

company_example.text: Anthropic open-sourced MCP in late 2024, and within months it was adopted by Cursor, Zed, Sourcegraph, and dozens of other AI tools. A single MCP server for GitHub, for example, lets any MCP-compatible IDE agent create PRs, review code, and manage issues without app-specific plugins.
